title: Vectors in Space
blurb: The third coordinate: 3D magnitude, arithmetic and dot products in space, the box diagonal, and normal vectors of planes.
buildsOn: Everything from Units 1–3 — each formula grows one more term.

lessons.title: Coordinates in 3D
lessons.concreteComparison: A drone needs three numbers: how far east, how far north — and how HIGH. Space vectors are plane vectors with one more lane, $(x, y, z)$, and every formula you know grows exactly one more term: magnitude becomes $\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$, and nothing else changes.
lessons.objective: Work with 3D components: read vectors between points, compute magnitudes, and solve for unknown components.
concept: A vector in space is a triple $(x, y, z)$. Between points, the rule is unchanged: $\overrightarrow{AB} = B - A$, now in three lanes.
Magnitude grows one term: $$|\vec{v}| = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}.$$ It's Pythagoras twice — first across the floor, then up the height — collapsed into one formula.

Everything you practiced in the plane transfers verbatim: unknown components come from $x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = |\vec{v}|^2$ (keep both signs!), parallel still means scalar multiple, and there are 3D integer families too — $(1, 2, 2)$ has length $3$, $(2, 3, 6)$ has length $7$, $(3, 4, 12)$ has length $13$.
lessons.keyIdea: Space = one more lane: AB = B − A and |v| = √(x² + y² + z²); everything else transfers verbatim.

facts.title: 3D magnitude
facts.explanation: Pythagoras run twice — floor first, then height.

facts.title: Between points
facts.explanation: Tip minus tail, three lanes now.

facts.title: 3D families
facts.explanation: The space analogues of 3–4–5 — exams reuse them constantly.

workedExamples.statement: The vector $(x, 4, 12)$ has magnitude $14$. Find every possible $x$.

commonMistakes.text: Leaving the z-term out of the magnitude.
commonMistakes.correction: All three lanes square and add. Forgetting z reduces a space vector to its shadow on the floor — a different, shorter vector.
B − A componentwise means three separate subtractions, each with its own signs. Write them in a column; don't juggle them in your head.

tryIt.statement: Find the vector from $(0, 1, 5)$ to $(4, 3, 1)$ and its magnitude.

steps.title: One more lane
steps.body: Planes needed two numbers; space needs three: $(x, y, z)$ — east, north, up. The good news is total: every rule from Units 1–3 survives unchanged, each formula just grows one more term.

steps.title: Pythagoras, twice
steps.body: Walk the floor diagonal first: $\sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$. Then climb $z$ and run Pythagoras again: $$|\vec{v}| = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$$ Two right triangles, one clean formula — and 3D has its own integer families: $(1,2,2) \to 3$, $(2,3,6) \to 7$, $(3,4,12) \to 13$.
beats: floor diagonal, then height — Pythagoras twice

steps.title: Which family?
steps.explanation: $9 + 16 + 144 = 169 = 13^2$. Note the nesting: $(3,4) \to 5$, then $(5, 12) \to 13$ — families feed families.

steps.title: Column the subtractions
steps.body: Three coordinate subtractions juggled mentally is how signs die. Stack the points, subtract lane by lane on paper — five seconds that saves the problem.

steps.title: Why stop at three?
steps.body: The formula √(x² + y² + …) doesn't care how many lanes there are. Physics uses 4 (spacetime), data science uses thousands — and the 'length' of a vector of movie ratings is computed with exactly this lesson's arithmetic.

steps.title: What sticks
points: $(x, y, z)$: one more lane, same rules.
$|\vec{v}| = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$ — never drop the z-term.

lessons.title: Arithmetic & Dot Products in 3D
lessons.concreteComparison: Same machines, one more input slot. Addition adds three lanes, scaling scales three lanes, and the dot product picks up a third term: $x_1x_2 + y_1y_2 + z_1z_2$. The angle formula, the perpendicularity test, the sign classifier — every Unit 3 tool works in space unmodified.
lessons.objective: Add, scale, and dot vectors in 3D; test perpendicularity; and find exact angles in space.
concept: Addition, subtraction, scaling: all componentwise, all three lanes. Parallel still means scalar multiple — check all three ratios.

The dot product gains a term: $$\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v} = x_1x_2 + y_1y_2 + z_1z_2,$$ and everything built on it transfers wholesale: $\cos\theta = \frac{\vec{u}\cdot\vec{v}}{|\vec{u}||\vec{v}|}$, perpendicular ⇔ dot $= 0$, sign classifies the angle.
Space is where these tools earn their keep: you can't eyeball a protractor between two arrows floating in 3D, but the arithmetic doesn't even notice the extra dimension.
lessons.keyIdea: All Unit-3 machinery + one more term: u·v = x₁x₂ + y₁y₂ + z₁z₂; angles and perpendicularity work verbatim in space.

facts.title: Angle in space
facts.explanation: Unchanged — the formula never asks the dimension.

workedExamples.statement: Find the exact angle between $\vec{u} = (1, 1, 0)$ and $\vec{v} = (0, 1, 1)$.

commonMistakes.text: Dropping the z-term from the dot product.
commonMistakes.correction: All three lanes contribute. A missing z-term changes both the number and — worse — the perpendicularity verdict.

commonMistakes.text: Testing parallel with only two of the three ratios.
commonMistakes.correction: (2, 4, 6) ∥ (1, 2, 5)? The first two ratios agree (2:1) but the third doesn't — not parallel. All three lanes must scale together.

steps.title: Same machines, third slot
steps.body: Every operation is still componentwise — there's just one more component. The dot product reads $x_1x_2 + y_1y_2 + z_1z_2$, and everything you built on it in Unit 3 — angle formula, sign classifier, perpendicularity — runs in space without modification.

steps.title: You can't eyeball space
steps.body: In the plane, a sketch settles most angle questions. Between two arrows floating in 3D, intuition fails — but the arithmetic never notices the extra dimension. This is where trusting the formula over the picture becomes the skill.
beats: dot gains a z-term; formulas unchanged

steps.title: Three-term dot
steps.explanation: $2 - 4 - 6 = -8$ — negative, so these space arrows lean apart.

steps.title: Trust the arithmetic over the sketch
steps.body: 3D sketches mislead — foreshortening makes right angles look wrong and wrong angles look right. In space, the dot product is the ONLY protractor you should believe.

steps.title: Space has a second product
steps.body: 3D vectors also support a CROSS product u × v — a vector perpendicular to both inputs. It powers torque, magnetic force, and 3D graphics, and it only exists in dimensions 3 and 7. University bait.

steps.title: What sticks
points: $\vec{u}\cdot\vec{v} = x_1x_2 + y_1y_2 + z_1z_2$; angle and ⊥ tests unchanged.

lessons.title: The Box Diagonal
lessons.concreteComparison: Will a 1.2-metre sword fit in a 1 m × 0.6 m × 0.5 m shipping box? The longest thing a box holds is its space diagonal — corner to opposite corner, through the middle of the air. Its length is the 3D magnitude of the box's edge vector: $d = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c^2}$.
lessons.objective: Compute the space diagonal of a rectangular box and a cube, and solve for an unknown edge from a known diagonal.
concept: Put a corner of an $a \times b \times c$ box at the origin and the opposite corner sits at $(a, b, c)$. The space diagonal IS the vector between them: $$d = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c^2}.$$
The cube is the special case $a = b = c$: $d = a\sqrt{3}$. (Compare the face diagonal $a\sqrt{2}$ — the $\sqrt{2}$–$\sqrt{3}$ pair is worth memorizing.)
Exams run it backwards: given the diagonal and two edges, the third edge falls out of $c^2 = d^2 - a^2 - b^2$. And the sword question: an object fits (in principle) iff its length is at most $d$.
lessons.keyIdea: Space diagonal d = √(a² + b² + c²); cube: a√3 (face: a√2); run it backwards for a missing edge.

facts.title: Box diagonal
facts.explanation: The 3D magnitude of the edge vector (a, b, c).

facts.title: Cube
facts.explanation: All edges equal; the face diagonal is a√2.

facts.title: Backwards
facts.explanation: Known diagonal + two edges → the third edge.

workedExamples.statement: Find the space diagonal of a $3 \times 4 \times 12$ box.
Find the space diagonal of a cube with edge $5$.
A box has space diagonal $25$ and two edges $9$ and $12$. Find the third edge.

commonMistakes.text: Using the face diagonal when the problem wants the space diagonal.
commonMistakes.correction: √(a² + b²) crosses one FACE; the corner-to-opposite-corner trip needs all three squares. Read which diagonal the figure draws.
Face diagonal a√2 (two edges involved), space diagonal a√3 (three). Count the dimensions the diagonal crosses.

tryIt.statement: Find the space diagonal of a $2 \times 3 \times 6$ box.
A cube's space diagonal is $6\sqrt{3}$. Find its edge and volume.

steps.title: The longest thing a box holds
steps.body: Corner to opposite corner, through the air — the space diagonal is the box's longest interior segment, and the answer to every "will it fit?" question. It's nothing but the 3D magnitude from two lessons ago, wearing packaging.

steps.title: Edges in three directions
steps.teach: The box's far corner sits at $(a, b, c)$ from the near corner — so the space diagonal is the magnitude of that edge vector: $d = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c^2}$. Steer the dimensions and watch all three feed the diagonal.

steps.title: One box
steps.prompt: The space diagonal of a $1 \times 2 \times 2$ box is…

steps.title: Face or space?
steps.prompt: A cube has edge $4$. Its FACE diagonal and SPACE diagonal are…
steps.explanation: Two edges make the face diagonal ($\sqrt{2}$), three make the space diagonal ($\sqrt{3}$). Count dimensions crossed.

steps.title: Diagonal² is linear territory
steps.body: Work with $d^2 = a^2 + b^2 + c^2$ as long as possible — squared, everything is addition and subtraction. Root only at the finish line.

steps.title: Euler's brick is still missing
steps.body: A box whose edges, face diagonals AND space diagonal are all whole numbers is called a perfect Euler brick — and nobody has ever found one, or proven it impossible. The hunt has run for 300 years.

steps.title: What sticks
points: $d = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c^2}$ — corner to opposite corner.

lessons.title: Normal Vectors & Planes
lessons.concreteComparison: A tabletop is a plane; a flagpole bolted straight into it is a NORMAL — perpendicular to every direction lying in the surface. Algebra hands you the flagpole for free: the plane $ax + by + cz = d$ has normal $(a, b, c)$ — the coefficients ARE the perpendicular direction.
lessons.objective: Read the normal vector off a plane's equation, and test planes (and lines) for parallelism and perpendicularity via their normals.
concept: The plane $ax + by + cz = d$ has **normal vector** $\vec{n} = (a, b, c)$: perpendicular to every segment inside the plane. No computation — just read the coefficients. (The $d$ only slides the plane along its normal; it never tilts it.)
Normals turn plane geometry into vector tests: two planes are **parallel** ⇔ their normals are parallel (scalar multiples); **perpendicular** ⇔ their normals dot to zero.

A line is perpendicular to a plane exactly when its direction vector is parallel to the plane's normal — the flagpole condition. These three tests answer nearly every ЭЕШ plane question without a single picture.
lessons.keyIdea: ax + by + cz = d has normal (a, b, c); planes compare by their normals — parallel normals ⇒ parallel planes, zero dot ⇒ perpendicular planes.

facts.title: Read the normal
facts.latex: ax + by + cz = d \;\to\; \vec{n} = (a, b, c)
facts.explanation: Coefficients are the perpendicular direction; d only slides.

workedExamples.statement: Write the normal vector of the plane $2x - 3y + 6z = 12$ and find its magnitude.
Are the planes $2x - y + z = 3$ and $4x - 2y + 2z = 9$ parallel?
workedExamples.solution: Normals $(2, -1, 1)$ and $(4, -2, 2) = 2(2, -1, 1)$ — scalar multiples, so the planes are parallel (and distinct, since the equations aren't multiples: $9 \ne 2 \cdot 3$).

workedExamples.statement: Show the planes $x + 2y - z = 4$ and $3x - y + z = 7$ are perpendicular.

commonMistakes.text: Thinking d changes the plane's direction.
commonMistakes.correction: 2x − 3y + 6z = 12 and 2x − 3y + 6z = 40 are parallel copies — d slides the plane along its normal without tilting it. Only (a, b, c) sets the tilt.

commonMistakes.text: Testing plane-parallelism with a dot product.
commonMistakes.correction: Parallel planes need PARALLEL normals (scalar multiples / cross-ratios), not a zero dot. Zero dot means perpendicular planes — the opposite verdict.

tryIt.statement: Write the normal of $x + 4y - 8z = 2$ and its magnitude.
Find $k$ so that the planes $kx + 6y - 4z = 1$ and $3x + 9y - 6z = 5$ are parallel.

steps.title: The equation hands you the flagpole
steps.body: A plane in space is $ax + by + cz = d$ — and its **normal vector**, the direction bolted perpendicular to the surface, is just the coefficients: $\vec{n} = (a, b, c)$. No computation. The constant $d$ only slides the plane along the flagpole.

steps.title: Planes compare by normals
steps.body: Everything about how two planes sit reduces to their normals: parallel planes ⇔ parallel normals (scalar multiples); perpendicular planes ⇔ normals dotting to zero. And a LINE stands perpendicular to a plane when its direction runs parallel to the normal. Three tests, all Unit-3 machinery.
beats: read n = (a, b, c) off the equation

steps.title: Name the normal
steps.prompt: The normal vector of $3x - y + 5z = 8$ is…
steps.explanation: Coefficients, signs included: $(3, -1, 5)$. The 8 has no vote on direction.

steps.title: Perpendicular planes
steps.explanation: Dot: $2 + 2 - 4 = 0$ — the normals are perpendicular, so the planes are too.

steps.title: Translate the question into normals first
steps.body: Every plane-relationship question should start with one reflex: write both normals. The geometry vocabulary (parallel, perpendicular, angle between planes) is all answered by those two vectors.

steps.title: Your graphics card worships normals
steps.body: Every lit pixel in a 3D game is shaded by dotting a surface normal with the light direction — this lesson's two ideas, run trillions of times a day.

steps.title: What sticks
points: $ax + by + cz = d$: normal $= (a, b, c)$; $d$ only slides.
Planes ∥ ⇔ normals ∥; planes ⊥ ⇔ normals dot to zero.
Line ⊥ plane ⇔ its direction ∥ the normal.

practice.statement: Find the space diagonal of a $4 \times 4 \times 7$ box.
A cube has space diagonal $10\sqrt{3}$. Find its edge and surface area.
Write the normal of the plane $6x - 2y + 3z = 5$ and its magnitude.

testYourself.statement: The vector $(x, 6, 8)$ has magnitude $12\ldots$ does an integer $x$ exist? Find all real $x$.
Find the exact angle between $(1, 0, 1)$ and $(1, 1, 0)$.
A box has edges $2, 6, c$ and space diagonal $7$. Find $c$.
For which $m$ are the planes $2x + my - z = 3$ and $6x + 9y - 3z = 1$ parallel?
Vertices $A(0,0,0)$, $B(2, 1, 2)$, $C(-2, 2, 1)$. Show the angle at $A$ is right.

testYourself.solution: $4 - 14 + 25 = 15$: $\sqrt{15}$. (Works in any dimension — the expansion never asks.)
